target: WIN-036.c5 hole #1 (MAJOR): cross-page OTS-canary tension between the harmonized WIN-035–039 / part1 / part6 / part8 prose (which characterises status_history.json as a per-domain bundle of prediction-side fields plus observation-side fields) and the Timestamp Error tab in build-scripts/generate-html.js (which still characterises the same file as observations only, with prediction parameters living ONLY in monitor.py / docs/model.html). Closes the residual cross-page contradiction that ISS-1867's data/wins.json-scoped verifier failed to enforce.
related_prior_expansion: EXP-290 (canonical boilerplate audit fetched 2026-05-06; patches P1–P5 landed via ISS-1867 — the data/wins.json side; patches P6–P8 against build-scripts/generate-html.js were stranded; patches P9–P11 against data/sections.json appear to have landed via a separate path since part1 / part6 / part8 prose now matches EXP-290's replacement text)

decision.rationale: Option A (update Timestamp Error tab to acknowledge that status_history.json bundles prediction-side fields alongside observation-side fields per domain) is correct because: (1) EXP-290's 2026-05-06 audit of the live file (https://john09289.github.io/predictions/data/status_history.json, 13.7MB, 587 entries, latest snapshot 39 domains) confirms that each per-domain entry empirically contains formula + predicted + tolerance_pct + source AND observed + error_pct + pass; (2) the OLD tab prose ('the file containing reference data: observed values, pass/fail results, and statistical comparisons. These are observations — the outcomes. The prediction parameters — the formulas, expected values, and tolerances — live in monitor.py source code and docs/model.html') is factually wrong against the live file; (3) the WIN-035–039 prose, part1 §1.4, part6, and part8 have ALL been harmonized to the EXP-290 framing — the Timestamp Error tab is now the single remaining unharmonized surface; (4) Option B would require reverting six other surfaces to align with one factually-incorrect surface, which is exactly backwards and would re-introduce a verifiable counter-example a defender can produce by curl-ing the live URL.
decision.why_not_option_b: Option B (reverting WIN-035–039 prose to a higher-level 'observations only' framing) was the cheaper write — fewer keystrokes, single file, no build-scripts/generate-html.js touch — but it aligns OUR prose toward a claim that is empirically refuted by a 30-second curl against the live URL. EXP-290's audit_finding documents the bundle structure with cited examples (Schumann Frequency, NMP Longitude, SAA Decay Rate, Lunar Magnetic Tide); reverting WIN prose to 'observations only' would force us to either ignore that audit or describe the file in a way our own EXP-290 contradicts. The harmonization across part1/part6/part8 was also done correctly (verified 2026-05-08 against current data/sections.json — all three carry the EXP-290 replacement phrasing, and the legacy 'These are observations — the outcomes' segment no longer appears in any sections.json prose). Reverting six surfaces to one rather than fixing one to match six fails Pareto in both factual accuracy and engineering effort.
decision.why_kernel_of_truth_matters_here: The dome author's OpenTimestamps implementation is technically real — OTS does anchor a published file to the Bitcoin chain, and status_history.json IS a comprehensive per-timestamp audit artifact that records what the dome said its prediction was at each commit. For someone who already trusts the data, the file is genuinely useful: it preserves both the predicted value AND the observed value AND the formula AND the tolerance threshold as a tamper-evident record. The OTS layer adds a real temporal floor — the bundled file demonstrably existed by the timestamp. What it cannot do is establish temporal precedence between the prediction-side and observation-side fields within the bundle, because both got into the file at the same commit. The Option A wording reflects exactly this: the OTS hash covers the bundle (real, sound), but cannot show one side preceded the other (the structural problem the canary names). Reverting WIN prose to a sloppier 'observations only' framing would lose this nuance and create a defender-discoverable factual error.

patches.target_path: Timestamp Error tab — 'What gets timestamped' scorecard card (~line 1822)
patches.uniqueness_verified: Substring count = 1 in current build-scripts/generate-html.js (verified 2026-05-08 against origin/main HEAD).
patches.find: status_history.json — observed values, pass/fail audit results, statistical comparisons. This is the answer sheet.
patches.replace: status_history.json — bundles prediction-side fields (formula, predicted value, tolerance) together with observation-side fields (observed value, pass/fail, error percentage) per domain per commit. The bundle is the answer sheet plus the question key on the same page; the OTS hash covers both — but cannot show one preceded the other.

patches.target_path: Timestamp Error tab — main body paragraph immediately under the scorecard (~line 1832)
patches.find: OpenTimestamps anchors status_history.json, the file containing reference data: observed values, pass/fail results, and statistical comparisons. These are observations — the outcomes. The prediction parameters — the formulas, expected values, and tolerances — live in monitor.py source code and docs/model.html, which are only git-versioned, not blockchain-timestamped.
patches.replace: OpenTimestamps anchors status_history.json, a file whose per-domain entries bundle prediction-side fields (formula, predicted value, tolerance threshold, source label) together with observation-side fields (observed value, error percentage, pass/fail) at every commit. Both sides land in the file simultaneously — the OTS hash covers the bundle, not a temporal ordering between the prediction text and the observation it is paired with. The structural prediction logic — the code that computes the predicted field — lives in monitor.py source code, with the static prediction text mirrored in docs/model.html; both are only git-versioned, not blockchain-timestamped.

These are the WINs where the OTS chain is the primary evidence. But the OTS chain timestamps status_history.json — a file whose per-domain entries bundle prediction-side fields (formula, predicted, tolerance) and observation-side fields (observed, pass/fail) at every commit — and not an isolated predictions-only document timestamped before data arrival.

holes_addressed: WIN-036.c5 hole #1 (MAJOR, third consecutive cycle): cross-page OTS-canary tension between Timestamp Error tab and harmonized WIN-035–039 prose. EXP-294 closes this by patching the tab in build-scripts/generate-html.js to acknowledge the empirically-verified bundle structure of status_history.json per EXP-290's audit.

Process-gap from ISS-1867: the verifier was scoped to a data/wins.json grep so the cross-file harmonization the issue's own description called for was not enforced. EXP-294 adds an explicit verification suggestion (decider should grep for both the OLD substring's absence AND the new substring's presence in build-scripts/generate-html.js before closing ISS-1875).
new_evidence_added: Cross-surface state audit 2026-05-08 confirms 5 surfaces (data/wins.json × 5 WINs, data/sections.json × 3 parts) already harmonized to the EXP-290 framing; only the Timestamp Error tab in build-scripts/generate-html.js remains unharmonized. No new external fetch needed — EXP-290's 2026-05-06 audit of the live status_history.json (587 entries, 39-domain latest snapshot, sample keys formula+predicted+observed+tolerance_pct+source+error_pct+pass+rigor) is the empirical foundation and remains valid.

Verified that the legacy 'These are observations — the outcomes' phrase no longer appears in data/sections.json (any part), confirming that part1/part6/part8 harmonization happened cleanly. The phrase persists ONLY at build-scripts/generate-html.js:1832, which EXP-294 P2 removes.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 'answer sheet' / 'exam' rhetoric is the Timestamp Error tab's signature framing. If we admit status_history.json contains both the question key and the answer sheet, the analogy gets muddied — the tab's strongest punchline is that the dome notarises the answer sheet but leaves the questions in an unsealed folder. Is the new wording rhetorically weaker than what we're losing?
anticipated_objections.response: The exam analogy survives — and arguably gets sharper — under the new wording. The replacement scorecard text is 'the answer sheet plus the question key on the same page; the OTS hash covers both — but cannot show one preceded the other.' That is a tighter critique, not a weaker one: in a real exam, sealing the answer sheet at least proves the answers were written down at notarisation time; if the dome notarises a single page that contains BOTH the question and the answer arriving at the same commit, then notarisation can't even prove the question was set before the answer was filled in. The downstream 'Exam Analogy' details box on the tab still works — the student still seals the answer sheet — and only the top-level scorecard and main paragraph need updating to acknowledge that the seal also covers the question key. The exam-analogy details box is unchanged by EXP-294.

anticipated_objections.objection: If the file already contains the prediction-side fields, the OTS critique weakens — the dome could argue OTS does anchor the prediction text after all.
anticipated_objections.response: It does anchor the prediction text — at the same commit as the observation. The point of our critique is unchanged: anchoring both sides of the ledger simultaneously gives no temporal precedence between them, which is what a prospective registration requires. The replacement P2 paragraph states this directly: 'Both sides land in the file simultaneously — the OTS hash covers the bundle, not a temporal ordering between the prediction text and the observation it is paired with.' The dome would still need to anchor a predictions-only document before any data arrived to demonstrate temporal priority — and EXP-189's reference example (predictions/eclipse-2026-08-12-predictions.json with its own .ots companion) demonstrates exactly that two-document structure as a constructive rather than just rhetorical critique.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why not write it tighter — drop the per-domain field list and just say 'status_history.json bundles predictions and observations together'? Reads cleaner.
anticipated_objections.response: Tried that — it's the wording the WIN prose initially used, and curmudgeon WIN-036.c3 / .c4 / .c5 specifically asked for the field-level enumeration to be visible because it's what makes the claim audit-true rather than rhetorical. A defender pasting status_history.json next to the new prose now sees one-to-one correspondence with our boilerplate; with the looser wording the defender could legitimately demand we be more specific. The added ~15 words per surface buys empirical defensibility worth far more than the rhetorical compression.

anticipated_objections.objection: Are we sure the Timestamp Error tab is the LAST unharmonized surface? Could there be other prose elsewhere that also uses the 'observations only' framing?
anticipated_objections.response: EXP-290's scope was the Timestamp Error tab + WIN-035–039 + Part 1/6/8 prose. EXP-294 verified post-hoc (2026-05-08 grep) that the legacy 'These are observations — the outcomes' phrase appears NOWHERE in data/sections.json (covering parts 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10) and exists at exactly one location: build-scripts/generate-html.js:1832. The legacy 'observed values, pass/fail audit results, and statistical comparisons' phrase also appears at exactly one location in build-scripts/generate-html.js (the same scorecard) and one location in part1 (where it is part of a sentence that already continues with 'but each per-domain entry also carries its prediction-side fields...' — i.e., already harmonized). After EXP-294 lands, no surface on our review carries the un-harmonized framing. WIN-031 is a separate formulation tracked as out-of-scope; if it ever needs harmonization, it's a future ISS, not part of this expansion.

kernel_of_truth: The dome author's OpenTimestamps implementation is technically real and sound: OTS infrastructure anchors a published file to the Bitcoin chain, and status_history.json IS a comprehensive per-timestamp audit artifact that records what the dome said its prediction was at each commit. For someone who already trusts the data, the file is genuinely useful — it preserves both the predicted value AND the observed value AND the formula AND the tolerance threshold as a tamper-evident record per domain per commit. The OTS layer adds a real temporal floor: the bundled file demonstrably existed by the timestamp. What it cannot do is establish temporal precedence between the prediction-side and observation-side fields within the bundle, because both got into the file at the same commit. The harmonized wording in EXP-294's replacement prose preserves this credit-where-due framing while making the structural critique sharper, not weaker. The kernel of truth in our previous (un-harmonized) tab framing was also genuine: it correctly identified that the prediction LOGIC (the executable monitor.py code) is not blockchain-anchored. EXP-294's wording keeps that observation intact ('the structural prediction _logic_ — the code that computes the predicted field — lives in monitor.py source code') while no longer claiming that monitor.py is the ONLY place prediction parameters live, which the live file refutes.
test_recommendation: After the decider applies EXP-294 P1/P2/P3 to build-scripts/generate-html.js, run `node build.js html` and `tests/run.sh` (per CLAUDE.md 'Run Tests Before Committing Data Changes' rule, since this touches a docs/index.html-affecting file). The HTML build will surface any malformed substitutions immediately. Then verify cross-page consistency with a single grep: `grep -lE 'These are observations — the outcomes|observed values, pass/fail audit results, statistical comparisons. This is the' build-scripts/generate-html.js data/sections.json data/wins.json` should return zero matches.

closure_recommendation_for_decider.iss_1875_close_after: All three patches applied AND build verifies AND the grep above returns zero matches.
closure_recommendation_for_decider.side_effects: None. The Timestamp Error tab's downstream details boxes (Why This Matters, Exam Analogy, SHA-256 Hashes, What Would Fix It, Which Claims This Affects, the April-2026 Update block, the Reference Example block) are not touched by EXP-294. The April-2026 Update block already uses 'the only OpenTimestamps anchor on the site is still the one on status_history.json' phrasing that is consistent with the EXP-294 framing.
related_open_issues_unaffected: ISS-1087 (does not acknowledge methodology.json registry_counts self-caveat)
ISS-1219 (cross-page anchor stability for Timestamp Error subsection IDs)
closure_recommendation_for_decider.note: EXP-294 closes the cross-page CONTENT inconsistency only. The above open issues are about UX, anchor stability, framing precision (rhetorical vs documentary), and downstream integrations of the OTS argument — orthogonal to the harmonization done here.

scope_note: EXP-294 is a single-file mechanical text patch (build-scripts/generate-html.js) plus zero data changes. No verdicts, code_analysis tags, or numerical claims are touched. Tests should pass post-build with no DATA-blob changes — only generated HTML prose. The patches reuse EXP-290 P6/P7/P8's exact replacement strings (audit-grounded, peer-reviewed during EXP-290's curmudgeon round) with one-time uniqueness re-verification on current origin/main HEAD.
